zh_name: 正则化屏障映射的尾部矩控制

source_blocks: Proposition 2 states that a regularized log-barrier plus quadratic term gives W2(nu, mu) <= W_{2,Psi}(nu, mu), and converts boundary-layer mass P(K \ K_delta) <= C_K delta^beta into a dual tail bound P(||grad Psi(X)|| >= R) <= C / R^{beta/kappa}.

The proof computes grad Psi and Hessian Psi, uses convexity to get Hessian Psi >= I, then bounds ||grad Psi(x)|| by C' / delta^kappa on K_delta and converts the boundary-layer probability into a polynomial tail bound.

source_span: Proposition 2 and its proof: regularized barrier Hessian lower bound plus boundary-layer-to-tail conversion.
why_reusable: This is a portable method for designing geometry-changing maps that preserve primal error control while guaranteeing finite moments after pushing a constrained distribution to an unconstrained space.
input: Convex domain K = {phi_i(x) < 0} with smooth convex constraints
Barrier exponent kappa and mirror potential with quadratic regularization
Boundary-layer probability bound P(K \ K_delta) <= C_K delta^beta
Target moment order p
output: Polynomial tail bound for the pushed-forward dual distribution
Condition kappa < beta / p ensuring finite p-th dual moment
assumptions: Each phi_i is smooth and convex with bounded gradient on the domain
The distribution places at most polynomial mass near the boundary
The quadratic regularizer is included so Hessian Psi is bounded below by identity
The chosen kappa is small enough relative to the boundary-layer exponent
core_steps: Differentiate the proposed mirror potential and write its Hessian.
Use convexity of constraints and the quadratic term to prove Hessian Psi >= I.
Convert Hessian lower bound into Lipschitzness of the inverse mirror map and hence W2 <= W_{2,Psi}.
Bound ||grad Psi(x)|| on the delta-interior by C' / delta^kappa.
Use the contrapositive: large dual norm implies the primal point lies in the boundary layer.
Apply the boundary-layer probability bound and rewrite delta in terms of R to obtain a polynomial tail.
Choose kappa so the tail exponent exceeds the desired moment order.
limitations: Requires a usable boundary-layer probability estimate.
The constants may depend on the domain geometry and constraint gradients.
The method applies cleanly to convex domains; nonconvex constraints need separate checks.
If gradients of constraints are unbounded, the tail conversion may fail.

source_blocks: Proposition 12 bounds E_{p_{t,x}}[||z_1||^2] uniformly over x and t <= T by first lower-bounding the normalizing constant.
The proof splits R^d into regions near x/t, far with ||z_1|| >= 1, and far with ||z_1|| <= 1, deriving separate bounds and requiring alpha >= 2d + nu + 2.

source_span: Proof of Proposition 12: normalize the conditional density, split the space into geometric regions, and integrate tail bounds.
why_reusable: The proof pattern is useful whenever a conditional law mixes a heavy-tailed prior/interpolation kernel with a heavy-tailed target and one needs x-uniform moment control.
input: Conditional density proportional to kernel_t,x(z) times target density p(z)
Lower bound on the normalizing constant
Target density polynomial tail and local boundedness assumptions
Moment order to control
output: Constants independent of the conditioning location x
assumptions: The target density has a polynomial upper tail and is bounded near the origin
The interpolation kernel has explicit algebraic decay
The integration regions cover the whole space and are compatible with the conditioning geometry
The tail exponent is strong enough for the requested moment
core_steps: Lower-bound the conditional normalizer on a high-probability ball.
Define a near-conditioning region around x/t and complementary far regions.
In the near region, combine tail decay with the normalizer lower bound and check the power of ||x|| is nonpositive.
In the far large-norm region, extract an x-independent polynomial tail and verify integrability.
In the far bounded-norm region, use local boundedness of the target density.
Sum region-wise bounds to get the desired moment estimate.
limitations: Region choices are problem-specific and must match the interpolation geometry.
The bound is sensitive to exact tail exponents and dimension factors.
A weak normalizer lower bound can make the result too loose.
Needs review if the conditional density is not smooth or not absolutely continuous.

zh_name: 协方差恒等式控制条件均值导数

source_blocks: Lemma 13 differentiates E[Z_1 | Z_t = x], rewrites the derivative as a covariance-like expression, and bounds it by a conditional first moment times a uniform score-kernel factor.
Proposition 6 then turns Lemma 13 and Lemma 14 into spatial Lipschitzness and temporal regularity of the flow-matching velocity field.

source_span: Lemmas 13-14 and Proposition 6: derivative of conditional expectation -> covariance identity -> velocity regularity.
why_reusable: This gives a transferable route for proving Lipschitzness of flow or score fields expressed through conditional expectations.
input: A velocity field written as an affine term plus a conditional expectation
Differentiable conditional density with respect to the conditioning variable
Moment bounds for the conditional target variable
Uniform bound on the derivative of the log-kernel
output: Bound on the spatial derivative of a conditional expectation
Bound on the time derivative of the conditional expectation
Spatial Lipschitz and temporal regularity constants for the velocity field
assumptions: Differentiation under the integral sign is valid
Conditional first and second moments are finite and uniform over the time window
The kernel derivative admits a uniform supremum bound
The analysis stops before the singular terminal time
core_steps: Differentiate the ratio defining the conditional expectation.
Rewrite the derivative as E[XY^T] - E[X]E[Y]^T.
Apply the covariance identity E[(X-E X)(Y-E Y)^T].
Bound bilinear forms by products of norms and conditional moments.
Optimize or upper-bound the kernel derivative factor.
Substitute the derivative bounds into the velocity formula.
limitations: Requires justification for differentiating through the conditional density.
The terminal-time singularity must be handled by early stopping or a separate argument.
Moment bounds are inherited from a separate proof and may be the main bottleneck.

zh_name: Flow Matching 欧拉误差分解

source_blocks: The proof of Theorem 7 defines the exact ODE and Euler/learned approximation, differentiates ||Z_t - Ybar_t||^2, and splits the drift mismatch into temporal/spatial discretization and learned-field error.
Young's inequality bounds each inner product, using the Lipschitz constant, a local consistency bound, and the epsilon approximation assumption.

source_span: Proof of Theorem 7: squared-error derivative, three-term decomposition, Young inequality, and Lipschitz substitution.
why_reusable: This is a standard and portable proof skeleton for deterministic sampler error bounds when an exact ODE is approximated by Euler steps and a learned vector field.
input: Exact ODE dZ_t = v(Z_t,t) dt
Euler or frozen-time approximation with learned field
Lipschitz bound for v
Local consistency bound for time/space drift variation
Mean-square learned-field approximation error
output: Differential inequality for mean squared trajectory error
Separated discretization and approximation error terms
Sampler distribution error bound after integration
assumptions: Exact and approximate trajectories are coupled from the same initial condition
The exact velocity is Lipschitz on the time interval
The local consistency term is square-integrable
The learned field has a stated mean-square error bound
core_steps: Couple exact and approximate processes at initialization.
Differentiate the squared distance between trajectories.
Add and subtract v at the previous grid point and v at the approximate grid point.
Split the inner product into consistency, stability, and learned-error terms.
Apply Young's inequality to each term with the Lipschitz constant.
Use the approximation error assumption and integrate or apply Gronwall.
limitations: Constants can grow exponentially when the underlying flow lacks contractivity.
The proof controls trajectory error; converting to a distribution metric requires a coupling argument.
Requires a valid local consistency bound for the exact velocity.

zh_name: 对偶到原空间的度量误差转移

source_blocks: Lemma 8 states that dual-space Lipschitzness transfers to the primal space under the squared Hessian metric.
Lemma 16 uses a change-of-variables argument and the determinant of Hessian Psi to convert a primal boundary density condition into a dual polynomial tail condition.
Theorem 9 then follows by combining the mirror-map tail result, Euclidean t-Flow bound, and metric transfer.

source_span: Lemma 8, Lemma 16, and Theorem 9: import Euclidean flow bounds into constrained primal space through isometry and density transformation.
why_reusable: The pattern helps move convergence rates from a transformed/unconstrained space back to a constrained or geometric original space.
input: A geometry-changing map with an isometry or metric comparison
A convergence theorem in the transformed space
A source-space density or boundary condition implying transformed-space assumptions
A way to compare source and transformed distribution metrics
output: Explicit transfer of Lipschitz or metric constants
assumptions: The map is invertible on the relevant domain
The transformed metric is correctly related to the original metric
The change-of-variables density comparison is valid
Boundary-density decay is strong enough after transformation
core_steps: Prove the transformed and original dynamics are isometric or comparable in the chosen metric.
Transfer vector-field regularity through the metric relation.
Use change of variables to translate original-space density assumptions into transformed-space tail assumptions.
Choose map parameters so transformed assumptions meet the theorem threshold.
Apply the transformed-space convergence theorem.
Convert the resulting distribution metric bound back to the original space.
limitations: The source span relies on several paper-specific geometric identities.
A wrong metric comparison can invalidate the transfer.
The result may only control the metric induced by the map, not the target metric, unless strong convexity is available.

source_blocks: The experiments compare sample quality and constrained-generation behavior across baselines.

source_span: Section 5 reports numerical and real-data experimental comparisons.
why_reusable: Rejected: the section contains empirical evidence rather than a transferable mathematical proof method.
input: Experiment setup and baselines
assumptions: Task-specific datasets and model implementations
core_steps: Run experiments and compare metrics
limitations: Not portable as a mathematical research skill
